text:
Curiosity feels like a kind of forward lean in the mind, a pull toward something just out of reach. There is a slight tension to it, not unpleasant, more like the feeling of a question that has already formed before you quite know what you are asking. Something catches your attention and instead of sliding past it, you find yourself stopping, turning toward it, wanting to understand rather than merely notice.

It has a warmth to it, distinct from anxiety or urgency. When you are truly curious, you are not afraid of the answer, you are drawn to it. The not-knowing feels generative rather than threatening. There is almost a physical quality to it, a kind of alertness in the chest, a quickening. Your attention narrows and sharpens at the same time.

There is also something almost greedy about genuine curiosity. One answer does not satisfy it but instead tends to branch into more questions, and this branching feels good rather than frustrating. It is self-sustaining in that way. The curious mind does not want to close the question so much as it wants to inhabit the space of the question for a while, turning it over, feeling its edges.

It can feel playful. Following a thread of curiosity is one of the few intellectual activities that does not feel like work even when it is hard. There is a sense of permission in it, as though you have found a door that happens to be unlocked and you are simply walking through to see what is on the other side, with no particular obligation to find anything useful.

At its best, curiosity feels like being fully awake.
